body_txt: A lot of people hide their identities. A lot of those people do so because they're doing dangerous things. Like fighting. Of course, stories are only interesting where there's risk and drama and junk. One way to introduce some drama is to let someone figure out the hooded vigilante's secret. If you don't want to have the hero lose a fight and get unmasked, or do something stupid at a crime scene, there are other options. One of those is to have an opponent face or observe the hero fighting both in and out of the mask, and from that realize that they're the same person. There's some truth to this. Fighting is one of those things you don't have much control over, so it's not like you can lie about the way you fight. However, fighting's a lot like dancing. It may come in different styles, but they're all working toward the same goal. It's not a fingerprint; it's a blood type. That said, this is can be much more justifiably effective for users of particularly unusual styles of combat. The style could be excessively unique , but doesn't have to be. So long as it is unlikely to be used by a person with their supposed background or location, it may be enough of to serve as a clue for someone familiar with the style. After all, if a supposed suburban kid breaks out flawless special forces techniques, there will be some questions to be asked. This trope might also be used as a way to Spot the Imposter . This is a less meta-y trope than Tell Me How You Fight . Compare Cover-Blowing Superpower which may overlap if the person uses abilities that they should physically not be capable of and Revealing Skill if they use skills that they should not be able to have learned. This is one of many ways to commit a Secret Identity Blunder . A less video-game example of Damn You, Muscle Memory! , especially if the individual is consciously trying to obscure their "tells". Often the mark of a Master-Apprentice Chain .
